title: The One With The Birth

jokes.text: But when I spoke to her, she said she'd already passed the mucus plug. [GROANS]
jokes.setup: Ross shares an overly medical detail about Carol's labor
jokes.punchline: Someone's disgusted groan at the TMI moment
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy - Ross's clinical overshare about birth details gets natural disgusted reaction. Character-appropriate for anxious Ross.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: The placenta was still attached and—

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa! Ross! We don't need the play-by-play!

jokes.text: I'm gonna be in the waiting room, handing out cigars.
jokes.setup: Question about what Joey will do when he has a baby
jokes.punchline: Joey's old-fashioned, stereotypical father behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect Joey character moment - his simple, traditional view of fatherhood. Well-integrated but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: When I have a kid, I'm gonna be one of those guys who's really involved, you know? I'm gonna go to all the baseball games, help with the homework...

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: That's great, Joe.

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Yeah, and I'll teach him to throw a ball, and... and how to talk to girls.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: Joey, that's really sweet.

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: And when he comes home with a girl, I'll just be sitting there cleaning my gun.

jokes.text: Yes, Joey's made arrangements to have his baby in a movie from the '50s.
jokes.setup: Joey's old-fashioned cigar comment
jokes.punchline: Chandler's sarcastic observation about Joey's outdated approach
jokes.explanation: Excellent Chandler snark - sharp, economical, and perfectly captures both Joey's innocence and Chandler's wit.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: Oh wow, a cigar. You know, that's a really good way to tell the world 'I have no idea what decade I'm living in.'

jokes.text: It's only like $2 for the first contraction and then 50 cents each additional contraction.
jokes.setup: Ross worrying about Carol giving birth in a cab
jokes.punchline: Chandler treating contractions like taxi meter rates
jokes.explanation: Classic Chandler - absurd logical extension of cab fare applied to labor. Original, well-structured, very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: Hey, hey, hey! Eight to the inch!

jokes.text: What, it's okay when Chandler does it? You have to pick your moments.
jokes.setup: Ross attempts his own joke after Chandler's success
jokes.punchline: Monica's blunt assessment that Ross can't pull off Chandler's humor
jokes.explanation: Meta humor about comedy timing and character dynamics. Monica's sisterly put-down is economical and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Why did the chicken cross the road? To get to the other side! No wait, because it was... um... it wanted to prove it wasn't a number!

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: Ross, you can't do Chandler. Just stop.

jokes.text: I just thought we might be here for a while, so, you know, things might get musical.
jokes.setup: Monica asks why Phoebe brought a guitar to the hospital
jokes.punchline: Phoebe's whimsical logic about spontaneous musical moments
jokes.explanation: Pure Phoebe - her unique worldview where hospital visits naturally become musical. Perfect character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: Why did you bring a guitar to the hospital?

dialogue.speaker: Phoebe
dialogue.line: Well, you never know when you're gonna have a spontaneous musical moment. Like, what if someone needs a song? What if the baby comes out and it's all traumatized from being born, and I can soothe it with a little melody?

jokes.text: Hey, where the hell have you been? We stopped at the gift shop. I was looking at stuffed animals, and Susan wanted a Chunky.
jokes.setup: Ross frantically asking where Carol's been during labor
jokes.punchline: The casual revelation they stopped for candy during active labor
jokes.explanation: Great cringe setup - Ross's panic contrasted with their casual candy break. Ross's overreaction is character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Where have you been? You're in active labor!

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: We stopped for candy.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: You stopped for candy? During active labor?

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: They were out of the good kind.

jokes.text: Susan wanted a-- You're having a baby. A baby. You don't stop for Chunkys.
jokes.setup: Ross processing the Chunky revelation
jokes.punchline: His escalating outrage at the candy priority
jokes.explanation: Ross's neurotic escalation works well. The repetition of 'baby' and Chunky brand name adds to the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You're telling me that Chandler chose Chunky over Rachel?

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Chunky! A candy bar!

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: He picked a CANDY BAR over my sister!

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: What is wrong with him?! What is WRONG with him?!

jokes.text: I used to have that bumper sticker.
jokes.setup: Ross's passionate speech about not stopping for candy during labor
jokes.punchline: Susan's dry comment treating it like a bumper sticker slogan
jokes.explanation: Brilliant deadpan deflation of Ross's intensity. Extremely economical and original - treating his outburst as bumper sticker wisdom.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's a great bumper sticker.

jokes.text: I see you're nine months pregnant. That's a good start.
jokes.setup: Doctor's comment about thinking of having a baby
jokes.punchline: Stating the obvious pregnancy as just 'a good start'
jokes.explanation: Mild doctor humor - understatement of obvious situation. Functional but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Franzblau
dialogue.line: I've been thinking of having a baby.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: Well, that's a good start.

jokes.text: Each one's like a little party in my uterus.
jokes.setup: Doctor asking how contractions are going
jokes.punchline: Carol's sarcastic description of painful contractions as parties
jokes.explanation: Good sarcastic imagery contrasting party fun with labor pain. Memorable phrasing but not groundbreaking.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: How are the contractions coming along?

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: Oh, they're great! I'm having a party every five minutes. It's a real blast!

jokes.text: Fifty-nine seconds. Quartz. Ha. Swiss quartz. Ha, ha.
jokes.setup: Ross correcting the contraction timing obsessively
jokes.punchline: His watch brand pride during a medical emergency
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ross neurosis - turning medical timing into watch technology bragging. Character-specific but minor moment.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: It's not 'it's', it's 'it is'. The contraction is 'it's' with an apostrophe between the 't' and the 's'.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: Ross, your arm is falling off!

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Yes, well, it IS falling off. And look at my Rolex—still keeping perfect time.

jokes.text: Ice chips, just ice chips. They're at the nurses' station. I'll get them. No, I'm getting it. Be right back. No, I got it. I'm getting it.
jokes.setup: Simple request for ice chips
jokes.punchline: Ross and Susan's competitive fight over who gets to help
jokes.explanation: Standard rivalry comedy - their competition over simple tasks. Sets up their ongoing conflict but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: I need some ice chips.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Franzblau
dialogue.line: I'll get them for you.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: No, I'll get them for you.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: That's okay, either one of you—

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Franzblau
dialogue.line: I'm getting the ice chips!

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: No, I'm getting them!

jokes.text: I'm Carol's ex-husband's sister's roommate. I'm your roommate's brother's ex-wife's obstetrician.
jokes.setup: Rachel's convoluted relationship description to the doctor
jokes.punchline: Doctor one-upping with an even more convoluted connection
jokes.explanation: Clever escalation of relationship complexity. Well-structured call-and-response that highlights the absurd social web.

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: So Ross is my ex, but we have a son together, and now he's married to Emily, who is the sister of my boyfriend Tag, except Tag and I broke up, and now I'm dating Joey, but Joey is also sleeping with Chandler's girlfriend Monica, who is actually married to Chandler, and Ross is also Monica's brother...

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Franzblau
dialogue.line: I see. Well, my wife's sister is married to my wife's brother, who is my wife's other brother's roommate, and my wife's brother is having an affair with my wife's sister's best friend, who is also my therapist's ex-husband's current wife's cousin...

jokes.text: I want a baby. Not tonight, honey. I got an early day tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Monica's emotional declaration about wanting a baby
jokes.punchline: Chandler treating it like a casual request for sex
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection - Chandler's deflection mechanism interpreting deep desire as casual request. Great character work.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: I want a baby!

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: Okay!

jokes.text: Get up. Come on, let's get some coffee. Oh, okay, because we never do that.
jokes.setup: Monica suggesting they get coffee
jokes.punchline: Chandler's sarcastic observation about their constant coffee consumption
jokes.explanation: Meta joke about the show's Central Perk obsession. Moderately clever self-awareness but not groundbreaking.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: We should get coffee.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: Yes, because we haven't had coffee in like... four minutes.

jokes.text: Shoot, shoot, shoot. Or just fall down. That's good too.
jokes.setup: Joey apparently missing a basketball shot on TV
jokes.punchline: His sarcastic commentary when the player falls instead
jokes.explanation: Basic sports frustration humor. Joey's character comes through but the joke itself is fairly generic.

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Hey, hey, hey! Look at that! He's falling down! That's what I call a slam dunk!

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm pretty sure that's not how you're supposed to play basketball, but what do I know?

jokes.text: You know, he couldn't hit water if he was standing on a boat.
jokes.setup: Discussion about poor basketball shooting
jokes.punchline: Lydia's variation on 'couldn't hit the broad side of a barn'
jokes.explanation: Standard variation on a familiar saying. Serviceable but not inspired - relies on existing phrase structure.

dialogue.speaker: Lydia
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, she couldn't hit the broad side of a barn if she was standing inside it.

jokes.text: They couldn't hit a boat if-- Wait.
jokes.setup: Joey trying to reverse Lydia's insult about the Celtics
jokes.punchline: Joey realizing mid-insult that his version doesn't make sense
jokes.explanation: Perfect Joey moment - his attempt at clever wordplay falling apart as he realizes the logic doesn't work. Great character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well, the Celtics are so bad, they... they make the Knicks look like... wait, no, that doesn't work.

jokes.text: Uh, let me get the father. Hey! We need a father over here! We need a father! There's no father. Oh. Oh. Oh, sorry.
jokes.setup: Joey calling for the father when Lydia goes into labor
jokes.punchline: Learning there is no father and the awkward aftermath
jokes.explanation: Standard awkward situation comedy. Joey's embarrassment is relatable but the setup is fairly predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Where's the father? We need to get the father!

dialogue.speaker: Lydia
dialogue.line: There is no father.

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: What do you mean there's no father? There's always a father.

dialogue.speaker: Lydia
dialogue.line: Not in this case.

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Well... this is awkward.

jokes.text: [SINGING] They're tiny and chubby And so sweet to touch But soon they'll grow up And resent you so much Now they're yelling at you And you don't know why And you cry and you cry and you cry
jokes.setup: Phoebe playing a sweet song about babies
jokes.punchline: The song takes a dark turn about children growing up to resent their parents
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Phoebe moment - subverting expectations of sweet baby song with brutal honesty about parenting. Original dark humor perfectly integrated with character.

dialogue.speaker: Phoebe
dialogue.line: And then the baby grew up and resented his mother for the choices she made...

jokes.text: Thanks, Ross. Yeah. I'm paying you to stop.
jokes.setup: Ross sarcastically thanking Phoebe for the depressing song
jokes.punchline: Phoebe's matter-of-fact response about being paid to stop
jokes.explanation: Great deadpan follow-up to the dark song. Phoebe's literal interpretation of Ross's sarcasm is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Thank you so much for that incredibly depressing song.

dialogue.speaker: Phoebe
dialogue.line: Oh, you're welcome! Although, I should tell you - people usually have to pay me to stop.

jokes.text: No fair. I don't even have one. How come they get two?
jokes.setup: Monica seeing twins
jokes.punchline: Her childlike complaint about unfair baby distribution
jokes.explanation: Monica's competitive nature applied to babies. Character-appropriate but not particularly clever or original.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: Wait, they're twins? How is that fair? I've been trying to have a baby for years and she just gets two?

jokes.text: When we're 40, if neither of us are married... what do you say you and I have one? Why won't I be married when I'm 40?
jokes.setup: Chandler's sweet offer as backup plan
jokes.punchline: Monica immediately focusing on the implication she won't be married
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Monica's insecurity immediately zeroing in on the pessimistic assumption. Sets up the escalation nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: And if by some miracle you're not married by the time you're 40, I will marry you.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: What do you mean 'if by some miracle'?!

jokes.text: Is there something fundamentally unmarriable about me?
jokes.setup: Monica pursuing the marriage question
jokes.punchline: Her escalation to 'fundamentally unmarriable' as a character trait
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Monica's neurosis. 'Fundamentally unmarriable' is a funny phrase that captures her overthinking perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: So when are you getting married?

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: Monica, we've talked about this.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: I know, but I'm ready now. Aren't you?

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: Yes, eventually.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: Eventually? What does that mean? A year? Two years?

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: I don't know, Monica.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: You know what? You're fundamentally unmarriable. That's just who you are as a person.

jokes.text: Dear God. This parachute is a knapsack.
jokes.setup: Chandler trapped in escalating conversation about Monica's marriageability
jokes.punchline: His metaphor about being in an impossible situation
jokes.explanation: Excellent Chandler metaphor - original, economical, and perfectly captures his panic. The parachute/knapsack comparison is brilliant and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: I'm like a guy on a sinking ship who keeps rearranging the deck chairs. Monica's great, but nobody's buying tickets.

jokes.text: Rachel, what is the deal with you and doctors? Is, like, your father a doctor? Yeah. Why?
jokes.setup: Monica asking why Rachel is so interested in doctors
jokes.punchline: Rachel casually confirming Monica's guess
jokes.explanation: Basic revelation humor. The setup is obvious and the payoff predictable. Serviceable character detail but not funny.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: Why are you so interested in doctors?

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: I don't know what you're talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: Come on, you've been asking about every doctor you meet.

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: So... you like doctors!

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: Okay, fine. I like doctors. Is that so bad?

jokes.text: What do you mean, 'Joey who?' [MOUTHS] Joey who? Tribbiani. Joey Tribbiani.
jokes.setup: Lydia's mother asking who Joey is
jokes.punchline: Joey's hurt reaction that he needs introduction plus awkward full name clarification
jokes.explanation: Mild Joey ego comedy - his assumption of fame/recognition deflated. Character-appropriate but not particularly strong.

dialogue.speaker: Lydia's Mother
dialogue.line: Who is this?

dialogue.speaker: Lydia
dialogue.line: Mom, this is Joey.

dialogue.speaker: Lydia's Mother
dialogue.line: Joey who?

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Joey Tribbiani. We've met like... five times.

jokes.text: She's not much of a phone person.
jokes.setup: Lydia's mother hanging up abruptly
jokes.punchline: Joey's massive understatement about the rude hangup
jokes.explanation: Good understatement after rude hangup. Joey's attempt to be diplomatic is sweet and mildly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Wow, she hung up on you. That's a little rude.

jokes.text: Hey, Knick fan, am I interested in your views on fatherhood? Um, no.
jokes.setup: Joey offering thoughts on fatherhood to pregnant woman
jokes.punchline: Lydia's blunt rejection of unsolicited advice
jokes.explanation: Sharp putdown of Joey's presumption. Lydia's directness is refreshing and economically delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: You know, having a kid, it's gonna change your life. But in a good way. You're gonna have someone to love unconditionally.

dialogue.speaker: Lydia
dialogue.line: That's nice, but I didn't ask for your advice.

jokes.text: So is this her? No, this is a loaner.
jokes.setup: Roy asking if this is Lydia
jokes.punchline: Joey treating a human being like rental equipment
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist Joey moment - applying car rental logic to human relationships. Original and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Is this Lydia?

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: No, this is Joey. But you can rent me by the hour.

jokes.text: I wasn't by myself. I had a doctor, a nurse and a helper guy.
jokes.setup: Roy saying sorry she had to do this by herself
jokes.punchline: Lydia's practical inventory of her support system, reducing Joey to 'helper guy'
jokes.explanation: Good practical response deflating Roy's concern. Joey being reduced to 'helper guy' is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Yeah, Knicks by 10. They suck. Eh, they're not so bad.
jokes.setup: Joey asking who won the game
jokes.punchline: Lydia contradicting her earlier harsh criticism after her team won
jokes.explanation: Nice callback showing Lydia's fairweather fandom. Her attitude change after winning is relatable and character-consistent.

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: So who won?

dialogue.speaker: Lydia
dialogue.line: My team did! You know, I have to say, I was wrong about those guys earlier. They're actually pretty good.

jokes.text: What do you mean, 'back to Jordy'? We never landed on Jordy. We just passed by it during the whole Jesse/Cody/Dillon fiasco.
jokes.setup: Ross learning the baby name changed to Jordy
jokes.punchline: Ross's detailed tracking of their name negotiation history
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ross neurosis - treating baby naming like contract negotiation. 'Jesse/Cody/Dillon fiasco' is great escalating language.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You changed it? To Jordy? But we agreed on Emma!

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: I know, but I was thinking—

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: You were thinking? We had a spreadsheet, Rachel. A COLOR-CODED spreadsheet. With footnotes. I have three backup copies in a safe deposit box.

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: It's just a name—

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Just a name?! Do you remember the negotiation process? Seventeen meetings. I created a PowerPoint presentation. We eliminated Jordan because it's unisex, we ruled out Jordy because it's 'too casual,' and now... now you're telling me you picked Jordy?

jokes.text: You get to sleep with her. I get the cramps.
jokes.setup: Ross helping with Carol's leg cramp
jokes.punchline: Susan's bitter observation about unfair distribution of benefits vs. pain
jokes.explanation: Sharp insight into the unfairness of the triangle. Susan's resentment is economically and wittily expressed.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: She gets the massage, I get the nausea.

jokes.text: I'm trying to get a person out of my body here and you're not making it any easier. Now go!
jokes.setup: Ross and Susan fighting during labor
jokes.punchline: Carol's matter-of-fact description of birth as getting a person out
jokes.explanation: Good frustrated mother moment. The clinical description of birth as 'getting a person out' is both funny and accurate.

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: I'm going to push the baby out now.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: That's it? That's all you have to say?

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: What do you want me to say? I'm having a baby. It's coming out.

jokes.text: What is that? Something exploded. It's just her water breaking. Calm down, will you?
jokes.setup: Ross panicking at water breaking
jokes.punchline: His 'explosion' interpretation and Susan's exasperated correction
jokes.explanation: Standard birthing panic comedy. Ross's dramatic interpretation is character-appropriate but the setup is predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Oh my God, oh my God! The water just broke! We're gonna explode!

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: Ross, that's not what that means.

jokes.text: Look, Carol never threw me out of a room before you came along. Well, there's a lot of things Carol never did before I came along.
jokes.setup: Ross blaming Susan for Carol's behavior
jokes.punchline: Susan's loaded implication about their relationship
jokes.explanation: Susan's loaded comeback about sexual awakening. The implication is clear but not groundbreaking comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: This is all your fault!

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: My fault? Maybe if you'd been a better boyfriend, Carol wouldn't have needed to leave you for a woman.

jokes.text: There are children coming into the world in this very building and your fighting noises are not the first thing they should be hearing.
jokes.setup: Phoebe breaking up Ross and Susan's fight
jokes.punchline: Her concern about 'fighting noises' being babies' first impression of the world
jokes.explanation: Sweet Phoebe wisdom about protecting newborns from negativity. Her unique perspective makes a valid point humorously.

dialogue.speaker: Phoebe
dialogue.line: You guys, you guys! Stop fighting. Do you know what the babies' first impression of the world is gonna be? Fighting noises!

jokes.text: Don't make me do this again! I don't like my voice like this!
jokes.setup: Phoebe having to be stern with Ross and Susan
jokes.punchline: Her discomfort with her own authoritative voice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Phoebe character moment - her peaceful nature conflicts with needing to be stern. Original and endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Phoebe
dialogue.line: Ross, Susan, you need to listen to me. I'm very disappointed in both of you.

dialogue.speaker: Phoebe
dialogue.line: Why do I sound like my mother right now? This is freaking me out.

jokes.text: What are you gonna do, suck the door open?
jokes.setup: Ross presumably trying to force the stuck door
jokes.punchline: Susan's sarcastic observation about his ineffective method
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy setup with sarcastic commentary. Functional but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's gonna work. Just keep banging on it like that.

jokes.text: He's in a whole other place. Yeah. No, he's gone.
jokes.setup: Monica telling someone Ross is doing great
jokes.punchline: Her immediate contradiction escalating to 'he's gone'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Ross's mental state. Monica's quick shift from diplomatic to honest is character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: Ross is doing great!

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: Well, okay, he's doing fine.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: Actually, he's okay.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: He's... he's gone.

jokes.text: I'm only 26. I'm not even thinking about babies yet. [BABY COOING] [SOBBING]
jokes.setup: Monica denying interest in babies
jokes.punchline: Immediate emotional breakdown when hearing baby sounds
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic timing - Monica's denial immediately contradicted by her emotional response. Well-structured visual comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: I don't want babies. I'm not interested in having kids.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: *hears baby cooing sounds* Oh my God... *immediately tears up* I need a baby so badly!

jokes.text: Where have you been? Oh, I just had a baby.
jokes.setup: Monica asking where Joey's been
jokes.punchline: Joey's casual announcement of major life event
jokes.explanation: Good understatement - Joey treating birth like a casual errand. The misdirection works well with his character.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: Where have you been? I haven't seen you in days!

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Oh, yeah, I got married.

jokes.text: It's hard enough to get women to go out with me. Heh. Right, yeah, I've heard that about cute doctors.
jokes.setup: Doctor claiming difficulty dating
jokes.punchline: Rachel's sarcastic disbelief with subtle compliment
jokes.explanation: Standard flirtation setup. Rachel's sarcasm is well-delivered but the premise is fairly predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Franzblau
dialogue.line: I have trouble dating.

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: Oh please, you're a doctor. Women love that.

jokes.text: Ahem. Yeah. Gotcha.
jokes.setup: Doctor's awkward coffee analogy about his work
jokes.punchline: Rachel's polite but uncomfortable acknowledgment of the bad comparison
jokes.explanation: Basic awkward conversation moment. Rachel's polite discomfort is relatable but not particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: You know, my work is like a cup of coffee. It's hot, it's bitter, and it keeps me up all night.

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: That's... that's a really great analogy.

jokes.text: So is this her? No, this is a loaner.
jokes.setup: Roy asking if this is the woman having Joey's baby
jokes.punchline: Joey repeating his 'loaner' baby joke
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier absurdist moment. Still funny but relies on previous setup - good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Roy
dialogue.line: Is this the woman having your baby?

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: No, no, no. It's a loaner.

jokes.text: Everyone knows who you are. Who am I? There's Father's Day. There's Mother's Day. There's no Lesbian Lover Day. Every day is Lesbian Lover Day.
jokes.setup: Susan expressing her marginalized position in the family
jokes.punchline: Ross's sarcastic suggestion that every day celebrates lesbian relationships
jokes.explanation: Sharp response to Susan's complaint about recognition. Ross's comeback is both insensitive and funny - good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: I feel like no one in your family acknowledges me or my relationship with Carol.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? We celebrate your relationship every day.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's called Lesbian Visibility Day.

jokes.text: And here's this little baby who has like three whole parents who care about it so much that they fight over who gets to love it the most and it's not even born yet. It's just the luckiest baby in the whole world.
jokes.setup: Phoebe reflecting on the complicated family situation
jokes.punchline: Her sweet reframe of the conflict as abundance of love
jokes.explanation: Beautiful Phoebe wisdom moment - reframing dysfunction as abundance. Original perspective that's both funny and touching.

dialogue.speaker: Phoebe
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about my whole family situation, and I realize it's not so bad. I mean, sure, my mom had me with my stepfather, and then she married my biological father, and then... okay, it's confusing. But you know what? I have so much love in my life. Some people don't have any parents, and I have like... four. That's abundance!

jokes.text: Uh-- Oh, that's great. Anybody seen a nipple?
jokes.setup: Ross presumably trying to help with medical equipment
jokes.punchline: His awkward announcement about missing medical part
jokes.explanation: Basic medical equipment comedy. Ross's awkwardness with the term is character-appropriate but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: I can't find the, uh... the thing that goes... *makes vague gesturing motions* ...you know, the medical part.

jokes.text: Hi, I'm Ben. I'm hospital Worker Ben. It's Ben to the rescue.
jokes.setup: Hospital worker introducing himself as Ben
jokes.punchline: His superhero-style self-introduction and the name coincidence
jokes.explanation: Mild coincidence humor with the name Ben. The superhero introduction is endearing but not particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Ben. Ben Parker. I work here at the hospital.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: But by night, I fight crime as... Spider-Man!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, that's not right either.

jokes.text: Well, Susan, I see what appears to be a dark vent. Wait. Yes, it is, in fact, a dark vent.
jokes.setup: Susan asking what Phoebe sees in the vent
jokes.punchline: Phoebe's overly dramatic confirmation of the obvious
jokes.explanation: Good Phoebe moment - her dramatic announcement of mundane observation. The mock-serious tone works well.

dialogue.speaker: Phoebe
dialogue.line: Oh! Oh my God! There's a dead bird in there!

jokes.text: Wait! You forgot your legs!
jokes.setup: Ross presumably falling through the vent
jokes.punchline: Phoebe's concerned observation about his partial descent
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy setup. Phoebe's practical concern about the legs is endearingly literal.

dialogue.speaker: Phoebe
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is he okay? He's stuck half in, half out!

jokes.text: So anybody who's not an ex-husband or a lesbian life partner, out you go.
jokes.setup: Too many people crowding the delivery room
jokes.punchline: Doctor's matter-of-fact categorization of the unconventional family
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan acknowledgment of the unusual family structure. Doctor's clinical approach to the chaos is perfectly economical.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Franzblau
dialogue.line: Okay people, I need everyone except the father out of the delivery room.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: That would be me.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Franzblau
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm going to need you to wait outside too. You're making me very uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Let me ask you, do you have to be Carol's lesbian life partner?
jokes.setup: Doctor's exclusion rule
jokes.punchline: Chandler testing if he qualifies for the exemption
jokes.explanation: Perfect Chandler opportunism - trying to find a loophole in the exclusion rule. Character-perfect and economically delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: So the doctor said I can't have caffeine anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: But there's an exemption for people with my condition.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: So I asked him, 'Do I qualify?' And he said, 'No, you're just nervous.'

jokes.text: Oh! A head. Oh. It's huge. Carol, how are you doing this?
jokes.setup: Ross seeing the baby being born
jokes.punchline: His shocked observation about the baby's head size and impossible logistics
jokes.explanation: Standard birth amazement comedy. Ross's awe and confusion is character-appropriate but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God. That's not coming out of there!

jokes.text: What does he look like? Kind of like my Uncle Ed, covered in Jell-O.
jokes.setup: Monica asking what the newborn looks like
jokes.punchline: Ross's unromantic but honest description
jokes.explanation: Good honest newborn description. Ross's specific comparison is both gross and endearing - captures the reality of birth.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: What does he look like?

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Well, he's got Monica's eyes, and my nose, and he's got this weird, angry expression on his face...

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: Aww!

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Yeah, he looks like an old man. Like an angry old man who's mad that we woke him up.

jokes.text: How about Ben? I like Ben. Ben. How come you never mentioned Ben before? We, uh, just cooked it up. That's what we were off doing.
jokes.setup: The name suggestion and question about its origin
jokes.punchline: The revelation they made up the name to cover for being trapped
jokes.explanation: Good callback resolution using the hospital worker's name. The cover story works as both practical solution and character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Phoebe
dialogue.line: We made it up.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: You made up the name?

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: We were trapped in the cabin during the storm.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: We needed a story, so we invented the whole thing.

dialogue.speaker: Phoebe
dialogue.line: Yeah, we just made up a name to cover for being stuck there.

jokes.text: Susan, he looks just like you.
jokes.setup: Looking at the newborn baby
jokes.punchline: Rachel's comment to the non-biological parent about resemblance
jokes.explanation: Mild family awkwardness about genetics and relationships. The joke is gentle but not particularly clever or funny.

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: Oh my God, he looks just like you!

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: No, not at all.

jokes.text: I know. I still am one of these.
jokes.setup: Someone commenting on having one of these babies
jokes.punchline: Joey's observation that he still acts like a baby
jokes.explanation: Perfect Joey self-awareness about his childlike nature. Simple but character-perfect observation.

dialogue.speaker: Joey
dialogue.line: Hey, I still act like a baby and I don't have one of those things!

jokes.text: I will always have gum.
jokes.setup: Monica introducing herself as Aunt Monica to baby
jokes.punchline: Her promise of perpetual gum availability
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Monica aunt moment - her practical, caring nature expressed through gum availability. Perfectly economical and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: Hi! I'm Aunt Monica. And I'm gonna be the cool aunt who always has gum.

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: You're gonna love me.

jokes.text: And sometimes I'll want you to steal third, and I'll go like this.
jokes.setup: Ross teaching baby about his availability patterns
jokes.punchline: Randomly including baseball coaching in the touching moment
jokes.explanation: Sweet Ross moment showing his priorities and dreams for fatherhood. The baseball addition is endearingly specific.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Hey buddy, Daddy's here now. Daddy's always here for you.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Well, except Tuesdays and Thursdays when I coach little league.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: And the occasional weekend when there's a really important dinosaur thing.

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: But other than that, Daddy's here.

jokes.text: Hey, Ben. Nothing. I don't think that's his name.
jokes.setup: Chandler trying to get baby's attention using his name
jokes.punchline: Monica's matter-of-fact assessment of the failed interaction
jokes.explanation: Mild humor about newborn responsiveness. Monica's practical observation is character-appropriate but not particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: Hey, Jack! Jack! Come on, buddy, look at me. Jack!

dialogue.speaker: Monica
dialogue.line: He's three months old.

jokes.text: He doesn't do much, does he? Nope, this is pretty much it.
jokes.setup: Chandler observing the baby's limited activity
jokes.punchline: Ross's honest assessment of newborn entertainment value
jokes.explanation: Honest observation about newborn reality. The exchange captures the anticlimax of meeting a newborn after all the buildup.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: So what does the baby do?

dialogue.speaker: Ross
dialogue.line: Well, right now he sleeps most of the time.

dialogue.speaker: Chandler
dialogue.line: Oh, that's great! So it's like living with Monica!

tmdb_overview: As Monica's biological clock ticks away like a time bomb, Carol goes into labor. Ross, Susan and Phoebe end up getting locked in a closet just as Carol's about to give birth to the baby. And Rachel flirts with an OB/GYN.
insight: Season finale trades jokes for heart: 70-point humor index masks emotional weight over laughs.
